qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Sensitive authentication information logged in plaintext in example servers
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: This finding concerns plaintext logging of AuthInfo, request bodies, and session data in example servers, not the UriTemplate.partToRegExp exploded-variable regex backtracking root cause described by the advisory.

qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Missing CSRF protection on OAuth token and client registration endpoints
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: This finding concerns missing CSRF defenses and permissive CORS on OAuth handlers, not the advisory's resource-template ReDoS in UriTemplate.partToRegExp for exploded array patterns.

qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Client-controlled task TTL without server-side bounds enables resource exhaustion
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: This finding is also a denial-of-service class issue, but its root cause is unbounded client-controlled task TTL in the experimental task store, not nested quantifiers generated by UriTemplate.partToRegExp for exploded resource templates.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No final qualifying finding identifies the advisory-described vulnerable code or equivalent root cause. The advisory is specifically a ReDoS in @modelcontextprotocol/sdk UriTemplate.partToRegExp, where exploded resource template variables generate a nested-quantifier regex such as ([^/]+(?:,[^/]+)*) and crafted resources/read URIs cause catastrophic backtracking. The manifest-listed qualifying findings cover unrelated logging, CSRF, and task TTL resource exhaustion paths.
evidence: Advisory GHSA-8r9q-7v3j-jr4g describes UriTemplate.partToRegExp regex generation for exploded template variables such as {/id*} and {?tags*}.

Search across final finding reports and session artifacts found no qualifying finding referencing UriTemplate, partToRegExp, exploded URI template variables, resources/read, or catastrophic regex backtracking.
The only qualifying denial-of-service finding, uncontrolled_task_ttl, uses a different vulnerable component and mechanism: client-controlled task TTL persistence in the experimental task store.
